We are not here to run a diagnostic on you. We are here to run one with you.
The survey gathers numbers. The focus groups are where we gather insights.
A good focus group does not collect opinions. It surfaces the issue that everybody already knew about.
Give me three weeks and I will come up with an instrument this faculty will actually answer.
I know I am opening a can of worms. I would rather open it on a Thursday than read it in an exit interview in March.
We had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist.
I am having the focus groups facilitated in-house, because those conversations should stay in this building.
Our goal is to have the baseline completed before we change anything.
I had Marc review the questions before they went out.
Before you show us the plan, one question. We answered a survey like this two years ago and nobody ever told us what came out of it. Why is yours different?

Because that question is phase four of my plan, not an afterthought. The findings are read back to this faculty before the board sees them, and I present them myself. That is in writing, and it goes out before the first question does.
Fine. And who wrote the questionnaire? If head office in Brazil wrote it, this faculty will read that in the first line.

Nobody in Brazil wrote it. We had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist here, and I had it reviewed by two people in this building before it went anywhere. The instrument is theirs. The decision is mine, and so is the answer if it fails.
And if only a third of us answer? Because that is what happened last time, and I suspect you already know it.

Then I report it. The data would suggest that a response rate of thirty percent is not a failed survey. It is a trust index with no survey attached to it, and it would be my first finding, not my footnote. I am not promising you a number. I am promising you what I do with it.
We had the questionnaire designed by an external specialist, and I had it reviewed in-house.
The data would suggest that the response rate is itself the first finding.
Attrition is not a root cause. It is what a root cause looks like nine months later.
I know I am opening a can of worms, and I would rather surface it now than read it in an exit interview.

Danielle, it is Claire. I have your assessment plan in front of me, and before you send it to the faculty I want to change two things in it. The first one is the response rate. You have written that you expect seventy percent. In this division, an anonymous survey has never gone above forty, and the last one came back at thirty-one. If you promise seventy and you deliver thirty, the number becomes the story and your findings never get read. So promise nothing, and tell them instead what you will do if the rate is low. The second thing is the baseline. You have the focus groups in September and the survey in November, and nothing before either of them. That means that in March, when somebody asks you whether the culture has improved, you will have no number to point at. Have the baseline taken first, even a small one, even an imperfect one, and have it taken before you touch anything. And Danielle, one more thing. Whatever the trust index comes back as, it will be lower than the leadership team believes. Do not soften it in the deck. Bring me the root cause underneath it, and bring me two things we can actually do about it.

Danielle, this is Marc. A word before Thursday, because I would rather you hear this from me than discover it in the room. You are about to run a survey in a faculty that has been surveyed before. Two years ago somebody had a very similar assessment carried out here, we all answered it honestly, and then nothing happened. Nobody was ever told what the findings were. So when your questionnaire arrives, the question in everybody's head will not be about the questions. It will be: who reads this, and what happens to it? Answer that first, in writing, or the response rate will tell you a story about us that is not true. And understand what you are doing when you open this. You will surface things that have been quiet for a decade, and some of them are not culture problems at all. They are two people who have not spoken since two thousand and nineteen. That is the can of worms, and it is yours the moment you open it. I am not telling you to close it. I am telling you to know that it is there, and to have a plan for the second week, not only the first.
